summary: Jeff Atwood argues that asking users what they want is fundamentally flawed because people's stated preferences diverge dramatically from their actual behavior. He uses research showing consumers choose feature-rich products but become frustrated using them—the 'all-you-can-eat buffet problem.' The post advocates for observational testing over surveys, citing Amazon's A/B testing culture and Microsoft's telemetry program as models. Atwood concludes that whether through lo-fi usability tests or automated data collection, directly observing user behavior yields insights that questionnaires and expert intuition cannot match.
key_points: Users consistently overestimate how many features they want, then suffer 'feature fatigue' when actually using complex products
Asking users what they want is nearly useless for usability—you must observe actual behavior
A/B testing and telemetry allow 'silent observation' at scale, as demonstrated by Amazon's cart recommendations winning despite executive opposition
Cultural context matters: Korean consumers prefer complexity as a status symbol, showing that simplicity isn't universally valued
Even internal experts and intuition are unreliable—Amazon's SVP was wrong, and Microsoft's 'gurus' couldn't predict actual feature usage
money_quotes: What users say they will do, and what they actually do, are often two very different things.
You have to observe what users actually do.
It's impossible to see that you're creating a Frankenstein's monster of a product – until you attempt to use it.
Consumers' success or failure interacting with the prototypes tells us more than a thousand surveys, questionnaires, or focus groups ever could.
The culture of experimentation at Amazon, where data trumps intuition, and a system that made running experiments easy, allowed Amazon to innovate quickly and effectively.
I love users. Some of my best friends are users. But like all of us humans, they're unreliable at best.
In order to move beyond usability guesswork, there's no substitute for observing customers using your product.
key_insight: User research based on what people say they want is fundamentally misleading; only by observing actual behavior—through usability testing or telemetry—can you understand how your product is really used.
